title: Detection of replay attack
abstract: Detecting a replay attack on a voice biometrics system comprises receiving a speech signal; forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal; and identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on said autocorrelation. Identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack may be achieved by: comparing the autocorrelation with a reference value; and identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on a result of the comparison of the autocorrelation with the reference value, or by: supplying the autocorrelation to a neural network trained to distinguish autocorrelations formed from speech signals resulting from replay attacks from autocorrelations formed from speech signals not resulting from replay attacks.
claims:
Claims 1 . A method of detecting a replay attack on a voice biometrics system, the method comprising: receiving a speech signal; forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal; and identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on said autocorrelation. 2. A method as claimed in claim 1 , wherein identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack comprises: comparing the autocorrelation with a reference value; and identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on a result of the comparison of the autocorrelation with the reference value. 3. A method as claimed in claim 2, wherein comparing the autocorrelation with a reference value comprises: comparing at least one feature of the autocorrelation with a reference value obtained from at least one other autocorrelation. 4. A method as claimed in claim 3, wherein the at least one feature of the autocorrelation is a feature associated with a first negative maximum and/or a first positive maximum of the autocorrelation. 5. A method as claimed in claim 3, wherein comparing the autocorrelation with a reference value comprises comparing at least one feature from a first range of lag values of the autocorrelation with a reference value obtained from at least one other autocorrelation. 6. A method as claimed in claim 2, wherein the reference value is an autocorrelation obtained from a user during enrolment. 7. A method as claimed in claim 2, wherein the reference value is a property of autocorrelations obtained from multiple users. 8. A method as claimed in claim 1 , wherein identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack comprises: supplying the autocorrelation to a neural network trained to distinguish autocorrelations formed from speech signals resulting from replay attacks from autocorrelations formed from speech signals not resulting from replay attacks. 9. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein the received speech signal includes speech segments and non-speech segments, the method comprising: selecting the speech segments of the speech signal; and forming an autocorrelation of the selected speech segments of the speech signal. 10. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein the received speech signal includes voiced speech segments and unvoiced speech segments, the method comprising: selecting the voiced speech segments of the speech signal; and forming an autocorrelation of the selected voiced speech segments of the speech signal. 1 1 . A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal comprises: dividing the speech signal into frames of data; forming a respective partial autocorrelation of each of the frames of data; and averaging said partial autocorrelations to form said autocorrelation. 12. A system for detecting a replay attack on a voice biometrics system, the system being configured for: receiving a speech signal; forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal; and identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on said autocorrelation. 13. A system as claimed in claim 12, wherein identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack comprises: comparing the autocorrelation with a reference value; and identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on a result of the comparison of the autocorrelation with the reference value. 14. A system as claimed in claim 13, wherein the reference value is an autocorrelation obtained from a user during enrolment. 15. A system as claimed in claim 13, wherein the reference value is a property of autocorrelations obtained from multiple users. 16. A system as claimed in claim 12, wherein identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack comprises: supplying the autocorrelation to a neural network trained to distinguish autocorrelations formed from speech signals resulting from replay attacks from autocorrelations formed from speech signals not resulting from replay attacks. 17. A system as claimed in any of claims 12 to 16, wherein the received speech signal includes speech segments and non-speech segments, the system being configured for: selecting the speech segments of the speech signal; and forming an autocorrelation of the selected speech segments of the speech signal. 18. A system as claimed in any of claims 12 to 17, wherein the received speech signal includes voiced speech segments and unvoiced speech segments, the system being configured for: selecting the voiced speech segments of the speech signal; and forming an autocorrelation of the selected voiced speech segments of the speech signal. 19. A system as claimed in any of claims 12 to 18, wherein forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal comprises: dividing the speech signal into frames of data; forming a respective partial autocorrelation of each of the frames of data; and averaging said partial autocorrelations to form said autocorrelation. 20. A device comprising a system as claimed in any of claims 12 to 19. 21 . A device as claimed in claim 20, wherein the device comprises a mobile telephone, an audio player, a video player, a mobile computing platform, a games 

 device, a remote controller device, a toy, a machine, or a home automation controller or a domestic appliance. 22. A computer program product, comprising a computer-readable tangible medium, and instructions for performing a method according to any one of claims 1 to 1 1 . 23. A non-transitory computer readable storage medium having computer-executable instructions stored thereon that, when executed by processor circuitry, cause the processor circuitry to perform a method according to any one of claims 1 to 1 1 . 24. A device comprising the non-transitory computer readable storage medium as claimed in claim 23. 25. A device as claimed in claim 24, wherein the device comprises a mobile telephone, an audio player, a video player, a mobile computing platform, a games device, a remote controller device, a toy, a machine, or a home automation controller or a domestic appliance. 26. A method of speaker identification, comprising: receiving a speech signal; forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal; comparing at least a part of the autocorrelation with at least one previously stored autocorrelation; and identifying a speaker based on said comparison. 27. A method as claimed in claim 26, wherein the received speech signal includes speech segments and non-speech segments, the method comprising: selecting the speech segments of the speech signal; and forming an autocorrelation of the selected speech segments of the speech signal. 28. A method as claimed in claim 26, wherein the received speech signal includes voiced speech segments and unvoiced speech segments, the method comprising: selecting the voiced speech segments of the speech signal; and forming an autocorrelation of the selected voiced speech segments of the speech signal. 29. A method as claimed in any of claims 26 to 28, wherein forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal comprises: dividing the speech signal into frames of data; forming a respective partial autocorrelation of each of the frames of data; and averaging said partial autocorrelations to form said autocorrelation. 30. A method as claimed in any of claims 26 to 28, wherein comparing at least a part of the autocorrelation with at least one previously stored autocorrelation comprises: comparing a part of the autocorrelation over a range of lag values with at least one previously stored autocorrelation. 31 . A method as claimed in claim 30, wherein the range of lag values is a predetermined range of lag values. 32. A method as claimed in claim 30, wherein the range of lag values is determined based on said autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal. 33. A method as claimed in any of claims 30 to 32, wherein said range of lag values excludes lag values corresponding to lags shorter than a lag at which a first positive maximum in the autocorrelation occurs. 34. A method as claimed in any of claims 26 to 33, wherein the previously stored autocorrelation is an autocorrelation obtained from a user during enrolment. 35. A system for speaker identification, comprising: an input for receiving a speech signal; and a processor configured for: forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal; comparing at least a part of the autocorrelation with at least one previously stored autocorrelation; and identifying a speaker based on said comparison. 36. A device comprising a system as claimed in claim 35. 37. A device as claimed in claim 36, wherein the device comprises a mobile telephone, an audio player, a video player, a mobile computing platform, a games 

 device, a remote controller device, a toy, a machine, or a home automation controller or a domestic appliance. 38. A computer program product, comprising a computer-readable tangible medium, and instructions for performing a method according to any one of claims 26 to 34. 39. A non-transitory computer readable storage medium having computer-executable instructions stored thereon that, when executed by processor circuitry, cause the processor circuitry to perform a method according to any one of claims 26 to 34. 40. A device comprising the non-transitory computer readable storage medium as claimed in claim 39. 41 . A device as claimed in claim 40, wherein the device comprises a mobile telephone, an audio player, a video player, a mobile computing platform, a games device, a remote controller device, a toy, a machine, or a home automation controller or a domestic appliance. 42. A method of speaker verification, comprising: receiving a speech signal containing speech of a speaker; forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal; performing a first comparison of at least one feature from a first range of lag values of the autocorrelation with a reference value obtained from at least one other autocorrelation; identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on said first comparison; performing a second comparison of a part of the autocorrelation over a second range of lag values with at least one previously stored autocorrelation obtained from a user during enrolment; and determining whether the speaker is said user based on said second comparison. 43. A method according to claim 42, wherein the second range of lag values does not overlap with the first range of lag values. 44. A method according to claim 42, wherein the second range of lag values overlaps with the first range of lag values. 45. A method as claimed in claim 42, 43, or 44, wherein said first range of lag values corresponds to shorter lags than the second range of lag values. 46. A method as claimed in claim 45, wherein said first range of lag values includes lag values corresponding to lags shorter than a lag at which a first positive maximum in the autocorrelation occurs. 47. A method as claimed in claim 45 or 46, wherein said second range of lag values excludes lag values corresponding to lags shorter than a lag at which a first positive maximum in the autocorrelation occurs. 48. A system for speaker verification, comprising: an input for receiving a speech signal containing speech of a speaker; and a processor, for: forming an autocorrelation of at least a part of the speech signal; performing a first comparison of at least one feature from a first range of lag values of the autocorrelation with a reference value obtained from at least one other autocorrelation; identifying that the received speech signal may result from a replay attack based on said first comparison; performing a second comparison of a part of the autocorrelation over a second range of lag values with at least one previously stored autocorrelation obtained from a user during enrolment; and determining whether the speaker is said user based on said second comparison. 49. A device comprising a system as claimed in claim 48. 50. A device as claimed in claim 49, wherein the device comprises a mobile telephone, an audio player, a video player, a mobile computing platform, a games device, a remote controller device, a toy, a machine, or a home automation controller or a domestic appliance. 51 . A computer program product, comprising a computer-readable tangible medium, and instructions for performing a method according to any one of claims 1 to 8. 52. A non-transitory computer readable storage medium having computer-executable instructions stored thereon that, when executed by processor circuitry, cause the processor circuitry to perform a method according to any one of claims 42 to 47. 53. A device comprising the non-transitory computer readable storage medium as claimed in claim 52. 54. A device as claimed in claim 53, wherein the device comprises a mobile telephone, an audio player, a video player, a mobile computing platform, a games device, a remote controller device, a toy, a machine, or a home automation controller or a domestic appliance.
